copy.title: Knicks steal Game 2 in San Antonio. Leclerc dominates Monaco. Tech sells off hard.
copy.keyTakeaway: Knicks are one win away from a title after stealing Game 2 in San Antonio. Tech stocks got hammered as rates climbed. World Cup kicks off in five days.

todaysHits.sports: Knicks suffocate Spurs in Game 2; one win from championship.
todaysHits.markets: Tech sells hard: QQQ down 4.8%, rates spike.
todaysHits.golf: Poston leads Memorial at 9-under, chase is tight.
todaysHits.f1: Leclerc wins Monaco; Hamilton, Verstappen chase.
todaysHits.worldcup: World Cup opens June 11 — 32 teams, one trophy.
todaysHits.culture: Scary Movie previews strong; Wordle keeps the streak alive.

lead.headline: Wembanyama went nuclear. Knicks still found a way.
lead.whatHappened: Victor Wembanyama dropped 29 points and dominated inside, but the Knicks' balanced attack—with Karl-Anthony Towns, Mikal Bridges, and Jalen Brunson all chipping in 20 points—was too much to overcome. New York stole Game 2 by one point to go up 2-0 in the Finals.
lead.whyBullet1: Wembanyama proved he can carry a team offensively, but the Knicks exposed the Spurs' depth problem: when one guy goes for 29, nobody else steps up enough to win close games.
lead.whyBullet2: The Knicks' three-guard setup is working. Brunson and Bridges ran the offense while Towns cleaned up on the boards (13 rebounds), and that flexibility is hard to defend in a seven-game series.
lead.whatToSay: Wembanyama's a monster, but the Knicks have four guys who can beat you on any given night. San Antonio needs De'Aaron Fox and someone else to step up, or this gets ugly fast.

markets.mood: Tech stocks got hammered today as the Nasdaq fell 4.8%, with mega-cap chip names like Nvidia and Dell both down 6.6%, while the 10-year Treasury yield climbed 1.9% this week.
markets.whyBullet1: The Treasury move matters because higher rates make expensive growth stocks less attractive—that's why Nvidia and Tesla got hit harder than Apple, which trades on steadier cash flows. The math just changed against speculative bets.
markets.whyBullet2: Watch for the June jobs report on Friday—that's what actually moves the needle on whether the Fed cuts rates later this year or stays put. Everything else is noise until then.
markets.bringUp: Bitcoin dropped 3.1% alongside the selloff, which tells you this wasn't just a tech rotation—it was a broader risk-off day where people sold anything that looked expensive.

golf.headline: Poston leads Memorial; Gerard and Burns chase at Muirfield Village
golf.whyCare1: Memorial is one of golf's blue-bloods — Jack Nicklaus's course, elite field, and a major FedEx Cup points play.
golf.whyCare2: Muirfield Village doesn't forgive mistakes; scoring at nine-under through 54 holes means someone's playing genuinely clean golf.
golf.watchFor: Whether Poston can hold at Muirfield's brutal back nine, or if Gerard makes ground Sunday with momentum from his second-place spot.
golf.whatToSay: Poston's leading at the one place where consistency actually gets rewarded — no gimmicks, just ball-striking.

f1.headline: Leclerc wins Monaco. Hamilton second. Verstappen third.
f1.whyCare1: Leclerc finally converted his home race into a win, something he'd struggled with before at the circuit where he grew up watching F1.
f1.whyCare2: Hamilton's second-place keeps him in the championship conversation while Verstappen's third raises questions about Red Bull's pace in tight street circuits.
f1.watchFor: Whether Leclerc can build momentum from this confidence boost or if it stays a one-off on a circuit that suits Ferrari's setup.
f1.whatToSay: Leclerc finally got the monkey off his back at Monaco — about time someone did.

culture.topic: Aaron Judge Out With Rib Stress Fracture
culture.whatHappened: Aaron Judge got diagnosed with a rib stress fracture and the Yankees are reevaluating him in four to six weeks.
culture.whyItMatters: Judge is the Yankees' best player and they're already fighting for positioning — losing him for a month in early June hurts the stretch run badly.
culture.whatToSay: Judge finally breaks and the Yankees' window just got smaller.

culture.whatHappened: Scary Movie opened Thursday night with 7.5 million in previews, landing near Scream 7 territory as Masters of the Universe grabbed 4 million.
culture.whyItMatters: Horror comedies still have gas, and the fact that two franchise revivals are both moving the needle means studios will keep leaning on nostalgia over original concepts.
culture.whatToSay: Scary Movie's still got it — which says something about what audiences actually want right now.

culture.whatHappened: Eddie Redmayne as a freelance assassin — methodical, charming, completely untraceable — until one investigator gets close.

copy.finalSharpTake: The Knicks are one win away from a championship because their defense suffocates you into submission — San Antonio scored 104 points at home and still lost Game 2. New York's not flashy, just relentless. Meanwhile, tech stocks cratered another 4-6% today as Treasury yields kept climbing, which means the market's officially pricing in a harder landing than it did a week ago. The World Cup opens in five days and everything feels stuck between what was and what's coming.
